frontmatter.title: Adding Homework Assignments for existing Homework Assignments in the Course Management Platform
frontmatter.summary: Procedure for adding assignment questions to an existing homework item in the course management platform.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Courses/Adding Homework Assignments for existing Homework Assignments in the Course Management Platform.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Add questions to an existing homework in the course management platform.
- Outcome: The homework is open and contains the required assignments.
- Trigger: Homework content is ready to be moved into the platform.
- Frequency: Per homework update.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Course management platform admin access.
- Tools: Course management platform.
- Inputs: Homework link, question text, answer options, and point values.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to [https://courses.datatalks.club/admin/](https://courses.datatalks.club/admin/) and log in.

screenshots.caption: This is the course management admin login screen with credential fields highlighted. Confirm you are entering the admin portal before changing homework state or questions.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and click on “Homeworks”.

screenshots.caption: This admin dashboard highlights the Homeworks section. Open this area to edit existing homework entries rather than course pages or projects.

flat_steps.body_md: In this example we are going to add assignments in the Homework 2. Click on Homework.

screenshots.caption: This homework list highlights the target Homework 2 row and its CLOSED state. Use the course, deadline, and state columns to pick the exact homework to update.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to [https://courses.datatalks.club/](https://courses.datatalks.club/) and select the course that you want to add a homework into.

screenshots.caption: This public course list highlights the course where the homework will appear. Check the student-facing course page so you can verify the homework status after admin changes.

flat_steps.body_md: You will see that Homework 2 is closed. To open it go back to step 3 then step 6

screenshots.caption: This student-facing homework list shows Homework 2 still closed. Use it as the before-state that confirms why the admin state needs to be changed.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and go to “State”. Click on the dropdown option and select “OPEN”. Then click on the “Save” button.

screenshots.caption: This admin edit screen shows the State dropdown set to OPEN and the Save button. Change only the target homework state, then save so students can access it.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to the course and you will see that the course is now open. Click on it

screenshots.caption: This public course page shows Homework 2 now marked Open. Use this as validation that the state change is visible to learners before adding question content.

flat_steps.body_md:
There are no tasks and assignments so we need to add questions in it. We need to change TBA.

    Note: Alexey will send a link to the actual tasks for this homework

screenshots.caption: This homework detail page still shows `TBA` instead of real instructions. Replace that placeholder with the provided GitHub homework link so students can reach the assignment.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to the course management platform and scroll down to the Description section and replace TBA with the github homework link.

screenshots.caption: This admin description field shows the `TBA` placeholder in the homework record. Select this field to replace the placeholder with the actual homework URL.
This shows the GitHub homework URL pasted into the description field. Confirm the URL points to the correct course cohort and homework before continuing.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and click on “Add another question”.

screenshots.caption: This shows the empty Questions table and the Add another Question button. Use it to start creating platform questions after the homework description has been updated.

flat_steps.body_md:
Go to the github homework link that Alexey provided and scroll down to find the Questions. Copy the question.

    Note: We don't copy Q1. In this example we only copy “Embedding the query”

screenshots.caption: This GitHub homework page highlights the question title without the `Q1` prefix. Copy only the question text needed for the platform field, not the numbering.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to the course management platform and paste the question into the “Text” field. Select Multiple Choice for the Question Type and do not change the Answer Type.

screenshots.caption: This admin question row shows the copied question text and Multiple Choice type. Verify the question type before entering answers so grading behaves as expected.

flat_steps.body_md: Copy the possible answers in the Github homework link details.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub question section highlights the multiple-choice answer options. Copy the options exactly so students see the same choices as in the source homework.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste it on the “Possible Answers”. Then click on “Add another Question”.

screenshots.caption: This admin row shows the possible answers pasted into the answer field. Check each option is on its own line before adding the next question.

flat_steps.body_md: If the question is worth 2 points, change the value in “Scores for correct answer” from 1 to 2.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub question title shows that the item is worth 2 points. Use the point value from the source homework to set the score correctly in the platform.
This admin questions table shows the score field set to 2 for the matching question. Confirm higher-value questions are adjusted before saving the homework.

flat_steps.body_md:
If the question has no answer options (e.g., it requires a typed response), set Question type to Free form.



    Then select “Any” in “Answer type”.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub question example has no multiple-choice options and expects a typed answer. Use it to decide that the platform question should be Free Form.
This admin dropdown shows Free Form selected for the question type. Choose it for typed-response questions so learners are not forced into multiple-choice answers.
This admin row shows Answer type set to Any for a free-form question. Use Any when the platform should accept a typed response without exact-answer validation.

flat_steps.body_md: Do the rest of the questions until all are added in the course management platform.

prose.body_md:
Adding Homework Assignments for existing Homework Assignments in the Course Management Platform
This document shows the steps on how to add Homework Assignments for existing Homework Assignments in the Course Management Platform

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Adding Tags in MailChimp
frontmatter.summary: Procedure for creating or assigning Mailchimp tags used to trigger course signup emails.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Courses/Adding Tags in MailChimp.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: The process involves assigning a tag.
- Outcome: To send them link email
- Trigger: When someone signs up for a course.
- Frequency: Once per course cohort.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Mailchimp audience access.
- Tools: Mailchimp.
- Inputs: Course name, cohort year, and previous tag naming pattern.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to [Mailchimp](https://login.mailchimp.com/) and log in.

screenshots.caption: This confirms the Mailchimp login entry point and the fields needed before any audience changes can be made. Use it to verify you are signing into the correct Mailchimp account before creating course tags.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Audience”, then click on “Tags”.

screenshots.caption: This shows the Mailchimp navigation path from Audience to Tags, with both sidebar items highlighted. Check this state before proceeding so the tag is created in the correct DataTalks audience area.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Create new tag”.

screenshots.caption: This is the existing tag list and the Create new tag button. Review nearby tag naming patterns here before creating the new course cohort tag.

flat_steps.body_md:
Type the name of the tag and click on “Create”.
    Example: llm-zoomcamp-2025

    Note: Before creating a new tag, review the existing tags to ensure correct spelling. You can use a previous tag as a reference by copying it and update the date if needed.

screenshots.caption: This modal is where the exact course tag is entered and created. Verify the spelling and year in the Tag name field before clicking Create, because this tag will later trigger course automation.

prose.body_md:
Adding Tags in MailChimp
This document shows the steps to Adding Tags in MailChimp.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Configuring MailChimp + Airtable integration
frontmatter.summary: Procedure for configuring the Mailchimp and Airtable integration that processes course registrations.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Courses/Configuring MailChimp + Airtable integration.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: The process involves getting database from Mailchimp
- Outcome: To collect registrations from the students of the course.
- Trigger: After creating a tag and automation in Airtable.
- Frequency: Once per course cohort or new course setup.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Airtable course base and MailChimpPoller GitHub repository.
- Tools: Airtable, GitHub, GitHub Actions, Mailchimp.
- Inputs: Course tag and Airtable database, table, and view IDs.

flat_steps.body_md:
Go to [Airtable](https://airtable.com/). Find and click on the course.
    In this example is the LLM Zoomcamp Course.


    Note: In the airtable there are multiple tabs

    - *Grid view - All the emails that are already processed and tags are already added.*

    - *Unprocessed - Emails that are not added a tag. Means automation is not enabled yet.*

screenshots.caption: This identifies the target Airtable course base for the integration setup. Confirm the course base before copying IDs or enabling the poller.
This shows the processed and unprocessed views inside the course base. Use it to distinguish records already handled by Mailchimp from new sign-ups that the poller should process.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to Github Repositories [airtable -mailchimp-poller](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/airtable-mailchimp-poller)

screenshots.caption: This confirms you are in the `airtable-mailchimp-poller` GitHub repository. The integration lives in this repo, so verify the repository before editing configuration.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll and Click the “config.yaml” file. These are the automations that are enabled

screenshots.caption: This highlights `config.yaml`, where Airtable database, table, view, and tag mappings are stored. Open this file to add or enable the course mapping.

flat_steps.body_md: Click the pencil icon to edit the file.

screenshots.caption: This shows the edit pencil on the GitHub file view. Click it only after locating the relevant YAML section so the next edits are made in the correct config file.

flat_steps.body_md:
*The only automation that is enabled is when we add something to the Mlops ZoomCamp2025 tag. Then this puller periodically pulls the data from the Airtable and adds a tag to this data to the contacts in MailChimp.*

    In this example, we want to enable LLMZoomCamp. Click on the “#” sign and remove all 4 of them by clicking backspace in your keyboard

screenshots.caption: This shows the commented course block selected for enabling. Remove the leading comment markers only from the target course block so the poller starts processing that course.

flat_steps.body_md: If it's a new course and you don't see it, copy an existing one and then paste it at the bottom. Replace the tag.

screenshots.caption: This edited YAML block shows the database, table, view, and tag fields that define one course integration. Use it as the template when adding a new course mapping.

flat_steps.body_md:
To get the first information for “database” “table” and “view”. Go back to Airtable, check on the link and make sure if it's correct.

    Note: In an Airtable URL: Example:https://airtable.com/appXXXXXXXXXXX/tblYYYYYYYYYYY/viwZZZZZZZZZZZ

    - *The part after the first slash (/) following airtable.com is the Database ID*

    appXXXXXXXXXXX → Database ID

    - *The part after the second slash is the Table ID*

    tblYYYYYYYYYYY → Table ID.

    - *The part after the third slash is the View ID*

    viwZZZZZZZZZZZ → View ID


    To get the second information for “table”.


    To get the last information for “view”.

screenshots.caption: This Airtable URL highlights the database ID segment. Copy the `app...` value into the YAML `database` field for the target course.
This Airtable URL highlights the table ID segment. Copy the `tbl...` value into the YAML `table` field so the poller reads the right table.
This Airtable URL highlights the view ID segment. Copy the `viw...` value into the YAML `view` field so only the intended view is processed.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on Commit changes.

screenshots.caption: This shows the GitHub editor with Commit changes highlighted. Review the new IDs and tag in the YAML before opening the commit dialog.

flat_steps.body_md:
Click on Commit changes at the pop up window.

    Note: When the polar will run, it will know that it needs to look in this database and whatever it finds there, whatever context it finds there, it will assign this tag to these contexts.

screenshots.caption: This commit dialog is the final save point for the poller configuration. Commit to the main branch only after the Airtable IDs and Mailchimp tag are correct.

flat_steps.body_md: Execute an action: Go to “Action” and click on “Poller”.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub Actions screen shows the poller workflow search and result. Use it to find the workflow that will test or run the new course integration.

flat_steps.body_md:
Click on Run Workflow, then click Run Workflow again in the pop-up window.

    Note:
    This step configures the MailChimp Poller, which pulls data from Airtable and assigns each email a tag specified in the YAML file. This is the final step needed for the automation to work.

    Once set up, every time someone signs up using the form, the system will automatically assign the appropriate tag.

screenshots.caption: This shows the Run workflow control for the poller. Trigger it after committing so the configured course can begin assigning the Mailchimp tag.

prose.body_md:
Configuring MailChimp + Airtable integration
This document shows the steps to Configuring MailChimp+Airtable integration.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Creating an automation campaign in mailchimp for course sign-ups
frontmatter.summary: Procedure for creating a Mailchimp welcome automation that emails course registrants when a course tag is applied.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Courses/Creating an automation campaign in mailchimp for course sign-ups.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Create a Mailchimp welcome automation for course sign-ups.
- Outcome: Contacts receive the correct course email when the course tag is applied.
- Trigger: A course cohort needs automated welcome emails.
- Frequency: Once per course cohort.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Mailchimp audience and course resources.
- Tools: Mailchimp, Slack, Telegram, GitHub, YouTube.
- Inputs: Course tag, course links, image, start date, subject, and preview text.

prose.body_md:
Creating an automation campaign in mailchimp for course sign-ups
This document shows the steps to creating Course Databases in Airtable.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Creating Automation for Course Sign-ups in MailChimp
frontmatter.summary: Procedure for setting up Mailchimp automation that sends welcome emails to new course signups.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Courses/Creating Automation for Course Sign-ups in MailChimp.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: The process involves creating automation to send email when adding a tag to subscriber
- Outcome: To set up an automation that sends a welcome email
- Trigger: Someone registers for the course
- Frequency: Once per course cohort.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Mailchimp, Airtable, and MailChimpPoller GitHub repository.
- Tools: Mailchimp, Airtable, GitHub Actions.
- Inputs: Course tag, welcome email copy, Airtable view details, and course links.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to [Mailchimp](https://login.mailchimp.com/) and log in.

screenshots.caption: This confirms the Mailchimp login screen used at the start of automation setup. Sign into the correct Mailchimp account before duplicating or editing course emails.

flat_steps.body_md: On the side bar, click on “Automations”.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp dashboard highlights Automations in the sidebar. Use it to navigate away from campaigns and into the journey tools needed for course sign-up emails.

flat_steps.body_md: Click “All Journeys” and select “Classic Automations”

screenshots.caption: This automations view highlights Classic Automations under All Journeys. Choose this path because the process replicates an existing classic welcome-email workflow.

flat_steps.body_md: Click the 3 dot icon and select “Replicate”.

screenshots.caption: This journey list highlights the replicate action for an existing course automation. Replicate the closest previous workflow so settings and email structure carry over to the new course.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Edit Settings”.

screenshots.caption: This copied workflow screen highlights Edit Settings. Open settings first so the workflow name and metadata match the new course before changing content.

flat_steps.body_md: Rename and type in the name of the course and click on “Update Settings”.

screenshots.caption: This workflow configuration screen shows the course-specific workflow name and Update Settings button. Verify the course name and year here because it identifies the automation in Mailchimp.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Edit” to assign a tag.

screenshots.caption: This workflow overview highlights the trigger Edit link. Open it to change which Mailchimp tag starts the welcome email.

flat_steps.body_md:
Click on the dropdown and select the tag for the course.

    In this example is llm-zoomcam-2025

screenshots.caption: This trigger editor shows the tag dropdown with the course tag selected. Choose the exact course cohort tag so only new registrants for that course enter the automation.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Update Trigger” at the upper right corner.

screenshots.caption: This trigger editor shows Update Trigger after the tag selection. Save the trigger here before editing the email content so the workflow is connected to the right audience segment.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on the “Design Email”.

screenshots.caption: This workflow overview highlights Design Email. Use it to open the replicated email template and replace all old course-specific content.

flat_steps.body_md: Click the Edit pencil Icon.

screenshots.caption: This email builder highlights the content block edit pencil. Click the pencil for the block you need to update rather than changing unrelated template sections.

flat_steps.body_md: Replace the \[COURSE NAME\] and the \[START_DATE\], Then click on the “Save & Close” button.

screenshots.caption: This text block shows placeholders for course name and start date. Replace both values and save so the welcome message describes the correct cohort.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and click on the Edit pencil icon to add an image.

screenshots.caption: This image block edit control indicates where the course banner is replaced. Update the banner before linking it so the email visual matches the course being promoted.

flat_steps.body_md: In this case the image was in the github, click on the image and save.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub page shows the course image file and save option. Download the image from the course repository when it is the source asset for the Mailchimp email.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to Mailchimp and click on “Replace”.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp image settings panel highlights Replace. Use Replace to swap the copied workflow image instead of adding a second image block.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on Upload.

screenshots.caption: This content studio screen highlights Upload. Upload the downloaded course image so it becomes available for insertion into the email.

flat_steps.body_md: Select the image and click on “Open”.

screenshots.caption: This file picker highlights the local course image file. Select the correct downloaded image before opening it in Mailchimp.

flat_steps.body_md: Now, select the image and click on “Insert”.

screenshots.caption: This content studio view shows the uploaded image selected and Insert highlighted. Insert only after confirming the thumbnail matches the current course.

flat_steps.body_md: If this notification shows up, click on “Let’s fix it”.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp warning indicates the inserted image may be too large. Use the fix prompt so the email image is optimized before sending.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Save” on the pop up window.

screenshots.caption: This image editor shows the resized course banner and Save button. Save the optimized image so the email keeps the right visual without loading issues.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Link” to add a link.

screenshots.caption: This image block menu highlights Link. Add the link here so clicking the banner sends registrants to the course repository or landing page.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to Github course repo and copy the link.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub repository page shows the course URL in the browser. Copy this URL as the destination for the course image link.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste the link and click on “Insert”.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp link dialog shows the course URL field and Insert button. Confirm the URL is the new course repository before inserting it.

flat_steps.body_md:
To check if the link is working, right click on the image and click on “Open link in new tab”.

    Note: This is very important to do so we don't accidentally have the old links when we copy. Do this in other phrases that have attached links to it to double check.

screenshots.caption: This image context menu highlights opening the linked image in a new tab. Use it to test the banner link and catch any copied-workflow URL that still points to an old course.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and click on the Edit pencil Icon.

screenshots.caption: This email section highlights the edit pencil near the Slack invitation text. Edit this block to replace copied Slack links and channel names.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to Slack and copy the Slack course link.

screenshots.caption: This Slack screen highlights the course channel link in the browser. Copy it from the actual course channel so the welcome email sends learners to the right Slack space.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on the phrase to attach the link on then click on the link icon.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp text editor highlights the link icon for the Slack channel phrase. Select the intended phrase before inserting the new Slack URL.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste the link and click on “Insert”.

screenshots.caption: This link dialog shows the Slack channel URL ready to insert. Verify it points to the current course channel before saving the link.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to Slack and copy the Slack course channel name.

screenshots.caption: This Slack channel details panel highlights the course channel name. Copy the channel name exactly so the email text matches the linked Slack destination.

flat_steps.body_md:
Replace \[COURSE_CHANNEL\] and paste the Course name channel.

    Note: Paste using Ctrl + Shift + V to copy it in the same format. It's important that both links are replaced.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp text block shows the course channel placeholder replaced in the invitation copy. Check both the visible channel name and the attached link before moving on.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and hover your mouse to “Join course Telegram Channel” and click on the Pencil icon.

screenshots.caption: This email builder highlights the Telegram button edit pencil. Open this button block to replace the copied Telegram announcement link.

flat_steps.body_md:
Go back to Github course repo and scroll down until you see the “Telegram Announcements”,then right click and copy the link.

    Note: You can also get the other links in these github repo for the slack and the course channels.

screenshots.caption: This course README highlights the Telegram Announcements link. Copy this source link so the welcome email points to the official course announcements channel.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to Mailchimp and replace the Web address (URL). Then click on “Save & Close”.

screenshots.caption: This button settings panel shows the Telegram URL and Save & Close. Confirm the URL is pasted into the button destination before saving the block.

flat_steps.body_md:
Scroll down and hover your mouse to “Course Q&A Streams” and click on the Pencil icon.

    Note: In this case we already have two, but if you had only one just remove Launch Stream and keep Pre-course Q&A

screenshots.caption: This Q&A Streams block highlights the edit pencil and current stream entries. Use it to decide whether to keep both stream links or remove a placeholder for the current course.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to the DTC Youtube channel and select the course video.

screenshots.caption: This YouTube channel view highlights the relevant course live video. Select the correct course stream before copying any watch URL.

flat_steps.body_md: Edit the link to remove “&” and copy.

screenshots.caption: This browser address bar highlights the YouTube watch URL and the extra parameter to remove. Clean the URL before linking it so Mailchimp receives the stable video link.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to Mailchimp and paste it. Then click on the link icon to turn it into a link.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp editor shows the stream URL pasted into the text and the link icon highlighted. Turn the visible URL into an actual hyperlink before saving.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste the link and click on “Insert”

screenshots.caption: This link dialog shows the YouTube URL and Insert button. Confirm the cleaned video URL before inserting it into the Q&A stream entry.

flat_steps.body_md:
Do the same for copying the link from Youtube and paste it for the Launch Course. Then click on “Save and Close”.

    Note: Check if the link is working by opening it in new tab

screenshots.caption: This text block shows both stream links and Save & Close. Use it to verify the pre-course and launch stream links are present before leaving the block.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll down and hover your mouse to “A few other things” and click on the Pencil icon.

screenshots.caption: This email section highlights the edit pencil for the “A few other things” list. Edit this block to replace repository, playlist, and FAQ links from the copied automation.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to the Github course repository and copy the link.

screenshots.caption: This GitHub repository page shows the course repo URL. Copy it for the “course repository” link in the welcome email.

flat_steps.body_md: Go back to Mailchimp and click on the link then click on the link icon.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp editor highlights the course repository text and link icon. Select the exact phrase before opening the link dialog so only that item is updated.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste the link and click on “Insert”

screenshots.caption: This link dialog shows the repository URL ready to insert. Verify the course slug in the URL before applying it to the email text.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on the course playlist and go back to Course repo to copy the link.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp editor highlights the course playlist text. Use this as the next link target after the repository link is corrected.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to DTC Youtube Channel and look for the course playlist. Click on “View Playlist”

screenshots.caption: This YouTube playlists view highlights the course playlist. Open the correct playlist rather than copying a single video URL.

flat_steps.body_md: Copy the link.

screenshots.caption: This YouTube playlist page shows the playlist URL in the browser. Copy this page link for the email playlist item.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste the link and click on “Insert”

screenshots.caption: This link dialog shows the playlist URL before insertion. Confirm it includes the playlist ID and belongs to the current course.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to Github Course repo to copy the FAQ link.

screenshots.caption: This course README highlights the FAQ link. Copy this URL so the welcome email points learners to the right course FAQ resource.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to Mailchimp and click on the FAQ link then click on the link icon.

screenshots.caption: This Mailchimp editor highlights the FAQ text and link icon. Select the FAQ phrase before updating its destination.

flat_steps.body_md: Paste the link and click on “Insert”

screenshots.caption: This link dialog shows the FAQ URL and Insert button. Verify the copied FAQ link before inserting it into the final resource list.

flat_steps.body_md:
Click on “Save & Close” and click on the “Save And Continue” button.

    Note: Check if the link is working by opening it in new tab

screenshots.caption: This final email editor view highlights Save & Close and Save And Continue. Use this checkpoint to confirm all course-specific links are replaced before leaving the design step.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on the Next button.

screenshots.caption: This workflow step highlights the Next button. Continue only after the design step has been saved so the final review uses the updated email.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Edit”

screenshots.caption: This review screen highlights Edit for Email Details. Open it to update subject and preview text before starting the automation.

flat_steps.body_md:
For Email Subject, replace the Course Name and Course Year
    In this example is should be “Welcome to LLM Zoomcamp 2024”

    For Preview Text, replace it with the Course Name without the Zoomcamp
    In this example it should be “Thanks for signing up for our LLM course!”

    Then click on “Next” button.

screenshots.caption: This email information screen highlights subject and preview placeholders. Replace them with the course name and short preview text so recipients see accurate inbox copy.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Save and Continue”.

screenshots.caption: This design screen highlights Save and Continue after email detail updates. Save here to return to the workflow review with the final email metadata.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Email Details”.

screenshots.caption: This review screen highlights Email Details. Open it as a final check that trigger, subject, preview text, and reply settings are all correct.

flat_steps.body_md: Check if the details are correct and if everything is good then click on “Start Sending”.

screenshots.caption: This final review screen highlights Start Sending after all checklist items pass. Start the automation only when the trigger tag and email details match the current course.

prose.body_md:
Creating Automation for Course Sign-ups in MailChimp
This document shows the steps to creating automation for course sign-ups in MailChimp

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Disabling MailChimp+Airtable Integration
frontmatter.summary: Procedure for disabling a retired or paused course integration in the MailChimpPoller configuration.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Courses/Disabling MailChimp+Airtable Integration.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: The process involves disabling a course in Airtable and to back up the database from that course.
- Outcome: The course integration is commented out in the poller configuration.
- Trigger: A course cohort should stop sending automated sign-up emails.
- Frequency: When a course cohort is retired or paused.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: MailChimpPoller GitHub repository.
- Tools: GitHub.
- Inputs: Course integration entry in `config.yaml`.

flat_steps.body_md:
Go to Github Repositories [airtable -mailchimp-poller](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/airtable-mailchimp-poller)

    In this example is the mlops Zoomcamp Course.

screenshots.caption: This identifies the correct `airtable-mailchimp-poller` GitHub repository before editing automation configuration. Confirm you are in this repo and on the main branch so the course integration is disabled in the right place.

flat_steps.body_md: Scroll and click the “config.yaml” file. These are the automations that are enabled.

screenshots.caption: This shows `config.yaml` with the active Airtable/Mailchimp mappings. Use it to locate the course block that currently enables the poller automation.

flat_steps.body_md: Click the pencil icon to edit the file.

screenshots.caption: The pencil icon is highlighted on the `config.yaml` file view. Click it only after confirming the course block, because the next step edits production automation configuration.

flat_steps.body_md:
Select the integration you want to disable. In this example, we are going to disable the MLOps Zoomcamp Course.

    Type “# ” (hashtag followed by a space) on your keyboard before each entry. It will turn gray, indicating that the integration between mlops zoomcamp in Airtable that is collecting registrations and Mailchimp is now disabled.

screenshots.caption: This shows the course block commented out with `#` markers. Check that every line in the target integration block is greyed out so the poller no longer processes that course.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Commit changes”.

screenshots.caption: This is the edited GitHub file with the Commit changes button highlighted. Use it to confirm the commented block is still visible before opening the commit dialog.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on “Commit changes” at the pop up window.

screenshots.caption: This commit dialog is the final confirmation for disabling the integration. Keep the commit on the main branch and commit only after the target course block has been commented correctly.

prose.body_md:
Disabling MailChimp+Airtable Integration
This document shows the steps to Disable MailChimp+Airtable Integration.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Adding Events to Course Calendars
frontmatter.summary: Steps for adding events to course calendars to notify participants and ensure they are aware of upcoming activities, with guidance for the related DataTalks.Club workflow.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Events/Adding Events to Course Calendars.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Adding events to course calendars to notify participants and ensure they are aware of upcoming activities
- Outcome: This process ensures proper communication with course participants and maintains a well-organized calendar system for course-related events.
- Trigger: Whenever an event is scheduled for any course (e.g., Machine Learning Zoom Camp, Data Engineering Zoom Camp), it must be added to the corresponding course calendar.
- Frequency: Per course event.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Course Google Calendars.
- Tools: Google Calendar, Luma, YouTube.
- Inputs: Event title, date, time, course, stream link, and optional event page.

steps.body_md:
Open the Calendar Application, locate and click on the “block” with the corresponding time and date for the event.![](../../../images/events/adding-events-to-course-calendars/media/image3.jpg)
    Image note: The screenshot shows the Google Calendar time block for the course event. Click the correct date and time slot before opening the full event editor.
Another tab will open, add the name of the event on the first line.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the calendar event editor with the title field at the top. Enter the event name there so it appears clearly on the course calendar.

steps.body_md: Look for “Add location” and paste the standard youtube link: [https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Add location field in Google Calendar. Paste the standard DataTalks.Club YouTube URL there for course events streamed on YouTube.

steps.body_md: Go to [https://datatalks.club/events.html](https://datatalks.club/events.html) and select the event to be scheduled. It will bring you to the Luma site and proceed to copy the “About Event” description.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the DataTalks.Club events page leading to the Lu.ma event details. Use the Lu.ma About Event text as the source for the calendar description.

steps.body_md: Go back to the Calendar and Look for “Add description of attachments” and paste the copied description from Luma.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the calendar description area where the Lu.ma About Event text should be pasted. This gives course participants the event context directly in the invite.

steps.body_md: Look for the “calendar symbol” and select a course calendar relevant to the event ,such as *Machine Learning Zoom Camp* or *LLM Zoom Camp*.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the calendar selector used to choose the course-specific calendar. Select the matching course, such as Machine Learning Zoomcamp or LLM Zoomcamp, before saving.

steps.body_md: Verify the Selection, ensure the chosen calendar matches the course for which the event is intended, confirm the event details: title, time, location, and description are accurate and clear and click “Save” to finalize and notify calendar subscribers.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the completed calendar event before saving. Review the selected course calendar, title, time, YouTube location, and description so subscribers receive the correct event.

frontmatter.title: Create a calender invite for the guests speaker for an event
frontmatter.summary: Steps to create Google Calendar invites for event guests or speakers with the correct event details.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Events/Create a calender invite for the guests speaker for an event.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose:
- Outcome:
- Trigger:
- Frequency:

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access:
- Tools:
- Inputs:

Do first: [Select and propose a date for an event](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/1USXNWAriIlK_AmbHSIR0qt3e0RC0aJh8GCSUJbq7-5k/edit)

What: Sending calendar invites to speakers for events.

Why: To make sure the speakers and Alexey have the event in their calendars and have quick access to the necessary information.

When: Immediately after agreeing on a date with the speaker, even if the topic is not yet finalized.

steps.body_md: After the guest has confirmed the date, go to the Google Calendar: [https://calendar.google.com/calendar](https://calendar.google.com/calendar)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows Google Calendar as the starting point for creating the speaker invite. Use it after the guest has confirmed the date so the new event is created on the calendar, not in email or another tool.

steps.body_md:
Then, Select the date of the event.
    Note: don’t edit the “DataTalks.Club Event” event, create a new one. The “DataTalks.Club Event” event is a blocker so nothing else could be scheduled in Alexey’s calendar.

    Also you should cross-check the date/time with Alexey’s calendar – to make sure he’s available. See more in [Alexey's Calendar](../../../internal-admin/reference/alexey-s-calendar.md)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the calendar view used to check the selected date and time against Alexey's availability. Create a separate event instead of editing the DataTalks.Club Event blocker.

steps.body_md: After selecting the date, click "More options" to add information

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the quick event popover with the More options button. Opening the full editor gives access to guests, description, location, and permissions.

steps.body_md:
Next, enter the title of the event
    Use these titles for the event:

    - *\<NAME\> at DataTalks.Club Podcast* - for podcast

    - *\<NAME\> at DataTalks.Club Webinar* - for the webinar

    - *\<NAME\> at DataTalks.Club Workshop* - for workshops

    Where \<NAME\> is the name of the guest, e.g. “Elena at DataTalks.Club webinar”

    Note: for proposed events just add the word “Proposed - \<NAME\> at DataTalks.Club podcast”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the event title field using the guest-name format. It demonstrates how a title like "Elena at DataTalks.Club webinar" should appear in the calendar invite.

steps.body_md:
Then, add the date and time of the event.

    Usual time:

    - 12:30 Berlin time for podcasts and webinars

    - 16:30 or 17:00 for workshops

    - We can do it at different times per speaker’s request

    Have an allowance of 5 minutes before the event for preparation. Thus, for a podcast it would be 12:25.
    Make sure the timezone is correct (Europe/Berlin)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the date, start time, end time, and timezone fields in the calendar editor. Confirm the timezone is Europe/Berlin before sending the invite.

steps.body_md:
To proceed, enter the zoom link in "Add Location"

    The zoom link for events is always [https://zoom.us/j/94755324762](https://zoom.us/j/94755324762)


    Note: Ensure that the invite contains only the Zoom link and no Google Meet link to avoid any confusion.

    6.a In case a Google Meet link is included in the invite, remove it by clicking on the pencil (edit) icon of the event.

    Replace \<TODO\> with the actual podcast event sample

    6.b Click on the “X” icon.

    6.c Click the blue “Save” button.

    6.d On the pop up notification, click on “Send”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the location field populated with the standard DataTalks.Club Zoom link. This keeps the invite location consistent and avoids adding a Google Meet room.
The screenshot shows a calendar event that has an unwanted Google Meet conference attached. Use the edit view to remove that Meet link before saving.
The screenshot shows the X control next to the Google Meet conferencing block. Clicking it removes the Meet room so attendees use the Zoom link instead.
The screenshot shows the blue Save button after removing the Google Meet link. Save applies the conferencing change to the calendar event.
The screenshot shows the Google Calendar notification prompt after saving changes. Choose Send so guests receive the updated invite without the Meet link.

groups.title: Adding guests and Alexey, adding description

steps.body_md:
Don't forget to add the email of the guest(s) in "Add guests" and check the box "Modify events"
    Note: Make sure to add both accounts of Alexey:
    [alexey.s.grigoriev@gmail.com](mailto:alexey.s.grigoriev@gmail.com)
    [alexey@datatalks.club](mailto:alexey@datatalks.club)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Add guests area with Alexey's required accounts and guest permissions. Include both Alexey addresses and the speaker email, then allow guests to modify the event.

steps.body_md:
In the "Add description" box, add the zoom link and the podcast document for a podcast or the outline document for workshops.
    Follow this format

    Zoom link: [https://zoom.us/j/94755324762](https://zoom.us/j/94755324762)

    Podcast document: \<TODO\>

    Replace \<TODO\> with the actual podcast document

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the description field with the Zoom link and podcast or outline document link. Replace the placeholder with the real working document before sending.

steps.body_md:
Once done, review the necessary information about the event. Make sure we didn’t forget anything.

    And that the guest is also there (Note that on the screenshot they aren’t added)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the final calendar event review screen. Use it to check the title, Zoom location, description links, Alexey's accounts, and the speaker guest list before saving.

steps.body_md: After reviewing, click save

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Save action in the calendar event editor. Click it after the invite details and guest list are complete.

steps.body_md: Lastly, select "Send"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the prompt asking whether to email guests about the calendar invite. Select Send so the speaker and Alexey receive the event.

frontmatter.title: Create events on LinkedIn
frontmatter.summary: Steps to create and announce DataTalks.Club events on LinkedIn, with guidance for the related DataTalks.Club workflow.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Events/Create events on LinkedIn.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Create and announce DataTalks.Club events on LinkedIn.
- Outcome: The LinkedIn event is published with the correct details and post.
- Trigger: An event needs LinkedIn promotion.
- Frequency: Per event.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: DataTalks.Club LinkedIn page admin access.
- Tools: LinkedIn, Luma or Meetup, YouTube.
- Inputs: Event link, title, date, time, description, speaker details, and cover image.

flat_steps.body_md:
The first need you will be doing is to visit the DataTalksClub LinkedIn account- viewed as an admin.


    Next, click "Admin tools" and select "Create an event"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the DataTalks.Club LinkedIn page in admin view. Use the Admin tools menu from this page to start creating the LinkedIn event.

flat_steps.body_md: And then, click the drag-down button under "Event Format" and select "External event link"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Event Format dropdown with External event link selected. This format lets the LinkedIn event point attendees to the external DataTalks.Club event page or stream.

flat_steps.body_md:
Afterward, type the event name and select your timezone

    Note: In this example, the event name is "Monitoring Model Performance with Crowdsourcing”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the LinkedIn event name and timezone fields. Enter the event title and choose the correct timezone before setting the date and time.

flat_steps.body_md:
Next, select the start date and the end time of the event.


    For the External event link, paste the [Youtube URL of DataTalksClub](https://www.youtube.com/datatalksclub).

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows LinkedIn's start date and end time controls. Match these values to the source event so LinkedIn shows the correct schedule.

flat_steps.body_md:
And for the "Description", copy the description of the event from Luma or Meetup.

    Note: You can edit the format of the description of the event like justifications, spacing etc.


    And then, type the name of the speaker under "Speakers"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Description field in the LinkedIn event form. Paste the event description from Luma or Meetup and clean up spacing before moving on.

flat_steps.body_md:
After typing the name of the speaker, attach the banner of the event by clicking
    "Upload cover image"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Upload cover image button after entering speaker information. Use it to attach the event banner to the LinkedIn event.

flat_steps.body_md:
After clicking "Upload cover image", select the picture on your computer and click "open"

    Note: You can edit and crop the picture.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the file picker for selecting the LinkedIn cover image. Choose the correct local banner and open it for upload and cropping.

flat_steps.body_md: And then, click "Next"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the LinkedIn event preview with the Next button. Continue only after the cover image and event details look correct.

flat_steps.body_md:
After clicking, add more description for your event by typing in the space
    Provided and click “Post”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the final LinkedIn post composer for announcing the event. Add the announcement text in the provided space, then post it.

flat_steps.body_md:
After you announced it, comment on the posted event using Alexey’s Account.

    Note: Here’s are some example of variations of the comment:

    - “Cool stuff”
    - “Looking forward to the talk \<NAME OF THE SPEAKER\>”
    - ‘Looking forward to it!’
    - “Looking forward to the chat \<NAME OF THE SPEAKER\>”
    - “See you next week, \<NAME OF THE SPEAKER\>”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows an example comment on the published LinkedIn event from Alexey's account. Use a short, natural comment that references the speaker or event.

prose.body_md:
How to create events on LinkedIn
This procedure will show you the steps on how to create events on LinkedIn. For announcing events on LinkedIn, announce the event 2 days after the Luma or Meetup announcement if it's more than one week from the announcement day. If it is less than a week, announce it on the next day.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Getting Post Analytics in LinkedIn
frontmatter.summary: Collect LinkedIn post analytics for event or content promotion reporting, including engagement and performance metrics.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Events/Getting Post Analytics in LinkedIn.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose:
- Outcome:
- Trigger:
- Frequency:

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access:
- Tools:
- Inputs:

flat_steps.body_md: Log in to the DTC Linkedin account. On the left side bar click on “Analytics”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the DataTalks.Club LinkedIn admin sidebar with Analytics available. This is the entry point for exporting post performance data.

flat_steps.body_md: Select a relevant date range for the posts you need and then click Export.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the LinkedIn analytics date range controls and export action. Set the range to cover the posts needed for the sponsors statistics sheet before exporting.

flat_steps.body_md: Once the download is finished, open the Excel file.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the downloaded LinkedIn analytics spreadsheet opened in Excel. This file contains the post rows and metrics used in the lookup steps.

flat_steps.body_md: Navigate to the “All posts” tab or subsheet.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the All posts worksheet in the exported Excel file. Use this tab because it contains the individual post links and performance columns.

flat_steps.body_md: Open the LinkedIn post links provided one by one.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the post URL column used to open each LinkedIn post. Opening the post lets you copy recognizable text for matching it back to the spreadsheet row.

flat_steps.body_md: For each post, copy the first line of the content text.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the visible LinkedIn post content where the first line can be copied. That line becomes the search key for finding the matching row in Excel.

flat_steps.body_md: Switch to the Excel file, press Ctrl + F, and paste the line you just copied into the search bar.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows Excel's search box with copied LinkedIn post text. Use Ctrl+F to jump directly to the matching post instead of scanning the export manually.

flat_steps.body_md: Excel will jump directly to the row for that post. You can then scroll to the right in that row to find the impressions and engagement data needed for the workshop sheet.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the matched Excel row with the post's analytics columns available to the right. Read the impressions and engagement values from this row for the workshop sheet.

prose.body_md:
Getting Post Analytics in LinkedIn
This procedure will help you quickly locate LinkedIn engagement metrics to update the Sponsors Statistics Workshop Sheet. Following this process is much faster than manually scrolling through the LinkedIn feed.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Creating Events on Google Calendar
frontmatter.summary: Steps to create a Google Calendar entry for a DataTalks.Club event, with guidance for the related DataTalks.Club workflow.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Luma/Creating Events on Google Calendar.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Create a Google Calendar entry for a DataTalks.Club event.
- Outcome: The event appears on the DataTalks.Club events calendar with the title, time, YouTube location, and Luma link.
- Trigger: A Luma event needs a matching Google Calendar entry.
- Frequency: Whenever a DataTalks.Club event is added to the calendar manually.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Google Calendar access to the DataTalks.Club events calendar.
- Tools: Google Calendar, Luma, YouTube channel link.
- Inputs: Event title, date and time in CET, subtitle, Luma event link, and YouTube channel URL.

flat_steps.body_md:
On the [Google calendar](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/u/0/r), click the time and date of the event.

    Note: Make sure that the time and date should be on CET.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Google Calendar time grid where the event slot is selected. It helps confirm the calendar entry starts on the intended CET date and time.

flat_steps.body_md: Type the name of the event, select the event’s schedule (date and time). Click on “Time Zone”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Google Calendar event editor with title, schedule, and Time Zone controls. It indicates where to verify the event name and open the timezone settings.

flat_steps.body_md: Make sure that time zone is in Central European Time (CET), and click “OK”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the timezone dialog set to Central European Time. Confirming this prevents the calendar event from appearing at the wrong local time.

flat_steps.body_md:
Type in the DTC’s YouTube Channel link as the event’s corresponding location

    [https://www.youtube.com/datatalksclub](https://www.youtube.com/datatalksclub)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the location field populated with the DataTalks.Club YouTube channel link. This tells attendees where the event will be streamed.

flat_steps.body_md: And then, make sure to select “DataTalks.Club event” calendar

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the calendar dropdown set to DataTalks.Club event. This confirms the event will appear on the shared DTC calendar.

flat_steps.body_md:
On the description, follow this format:
    Subtitle of the event :

    We will stream on our YouTube channel: https://www.youtube.com/datatalksclub

    More information: (Luma Link)

    Example:
    From Picture to Podcast

    We will stream on our YouTube channel: [https://www.youtube.com/datatalksclub](https://www.youtube.com/datatalksclub)

    More information: [https://lu.ma/cxm63jhj](https://lu.ma/cxm63jhj)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the description field with the event subtitle, YouTube stream line, and Luma information link. It provides the exact structure attendees should see in the calendar description.

flat_steps.body_md: Once done, click “Save" and it should look like the image below.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the completed Google Calendar event after saving. It confirms the title, time, calendar, YouTube location, and Luma details are visible together.

prose.body_md:
How to Create Events on Google Calendar
This document shows the steps on How to Create Events on Google Calendar based on Luma Events.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Creating events (Webinar, Workshop and Podcast) on Luma
frontmatter.summary: Create Luma event pages for DataTalks.Club webinars, workshops, and podcasts with the correct description, timing, and registration details.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Luma/Creating events (Webinar, Workshop and Podcast) on Luma_.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose:
- Outcome:
- Trigger:
- Frequency:

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access:
- Tools:
- Inputs:

This shows the steps to create events on Luma based on speaker’s provided information which may be viewed through email.

flat_steps.body_md:
The first thing you need to do is go to Luma’s website and under the “Calendar” tab click [“DataTalksClub events”](https://lu.ma/home/calendars)

    Note: DataTalkClub events should be selected for it to appear in the common space and not in the personal one.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Luma Calendar tab with DataTalksClub events selected. Starting from this calendar keeps webinars, workshops, and podcasts in the shared DTC space.

flat_steps.body_md: Once done, click the plus button right beside “Events”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the plus control next to Events in the selected Luma calendar. Use it to start adding a new event to the DataTalksClub events list.

flat_steps.body_md: Then, click “Create New Event”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Create New Event choice in Luma. This opens the full event form for a webinar, workshop, or podcast.

flat_steps.body_md:
Click on “Theme” and the Font (Default) and color (Gray).

    Note: Make sure that the font should be consistent for all events.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Theme settings for the Luma event. It identifies where to keep the default font and gray color consistent with other DTC events.

flat_steps.body_md:
Enter the event details (Title, Event cover, schedule of the event, event location, description)
    Add the DTC’s YouTube channel under Event location: *[https://www.youtube.com/@DataTalksClub](https://www.youtube.com/@DataTalksClub)*

    Creating the cover: [How to use Figma for creating event banners](../../../sales/sponsorship/sops/how-to-use-figma-for-creating-event-banners.md)

    Note: Be careful with the timezone and the AM / PM format. Make sure that the schedule of the event is in CET timezone. It is advisable to change your Computer or Laptop’s timezone to Berlin to avoid confusion.

    Podcast: Monday at 12:30 Berlin Time, if guests from US can be (17:00) or 5:00pm CET

    Webinar: Tuesday at 12:30 Berlin time, unless otherwise agreed with the speakers

    Workshop: 16:30 or 17:00 for workshops, we can do it at different times per speaker’s request

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the main Luma event details form with cover, title, schedule, location, and description fields. It is the reference point for entering the speaker-provided event details and CET schedule.

flat_steps.body_md:
In the “Event Description” add the details of the event.

    Note: Make sure that the format has a space \[Subtitle - Name\].

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Event Description editor in Luma. It indicates where to add the formatted event details, including the subtitle and speaker information.

flat_steps.body_md: At the bottom of the description add “​​​​​​[DataTalks.Club](http://datatalks.club/) is the place to talk about data. [Join our Slack community](https://datatalks.club/slack.html)!”. Then click on “Done”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the bottom of the Luma description editor with the DataTalks.Club and Slack community links. The Done button saves the description content.

flat_steps.body_md:
If the event is sponsored, indicate it at the last part of the event description through this format:

    “This event is sponsored by \[SPONSOR\].”

    Note: In this example, the sponsor is dltHub.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the sponsor sentence added to the event description. It demonstrates where sponsorship disclosure belongs when the event has a sponsor.

flat_steps.body_md: Once done, click “Create Event” or “Update Event” (If you are editing the event already created)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the final event form action for creating or updating the Luma event. Use it after the event details, description, and settings have been reviewed.
The screenshot shows the Update Event control for an existing Luma event. It confirms where to save changes when editing rather than creating from scratch.

flat_steps.body_md: On the Event tab, scroll down and click “Add Host” to add Alexey as the host and organizer of the event, and then select “Alexey Grigorev” (alexey.s.grigoriev@gmail.com).

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Host section with Alexey Grigorev selected. This confirms the event has the correct host and organizer assigned.

flat_steps.body_md: Go to “Registration”, scroll down and under “Custom Questions”, add an additional question:

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Registration tab and Custom Questions area. This is where attendee questions are added to the Luma registration form.

flat_steps.body_md: Select “Options:

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the custom-question type picker in Luma. Choose Options when the answer should be limited to predefined choices such as Yes or No.

flat_steps.body_md:
In the Question, type in or paste:
    “Do you want to subscribe to the DataTalks.Club newsletter and receive updates about future events?”

    Under Options, type 'Yes' (press Enter) and 'No' (press Enter). Ensure the Selection Type is set to 'Single,' and toggle the Required button to On.
    Then click on the “Add Question” button.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the completed newsletter subscription question with Yes and No options. The Add Question button saves it to the registration form.

flat_steps.body_md:
If an event is sponsored, we will add two more questions to ask if they want to share their emails with the sponsors and for the Company name of the registrants Follow the steps in number 11-14 and use the question:

    “I want to share my email with the sponsors (\<NAME\>)”

    Note: We will be sharing these information to the sponsors after the event

    In this example the sponsor is Astronomer

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the sponsored-event question configured with the sponsor name. It captures whether registrants agree to share their email with that sponsor.

flat_steps.body_md: For the second question follow step 11 and select “Text”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows Text selected as the custom-question type. Use it for the company-name question because attendees need to enter free-form text.

flat_steps.body_md:
In the Question, type in or paste:
    “Company Name”

    Response Length should be “Short” and toggle the Required button to On.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Company Name question settings with short response length and Required enabled. These settings keep sponsor-related registration data complete and concise.

flat_steps.body_md:
After creating/updating the event, make sure to manually add the event to the Google Calendar
    See here: [Creating Events on Google Calendar (Luma Edition)](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HwptQpp9w_TihEf7szGL130eSorzY_e_K4jSzAG-rAE/edit)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot points to the related Google Calendar process for adding the event manually. It links the Luma event creation flow to the calendar update that follows.

flat_steps.body_md: Make sure to turn off the notification for the “New Guest Registered” , go to settings, click preference then uncheck the New Guest Registered.

frontmatter.title: Creating project of the week events in Luma
frontmatter.summary: Steps to create a multi-day Luma event series for Project of the Week, with guidance for the related DataTalks.Club workflow.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Luma/Creating project of the week events in Luma.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Create a multi-day Luma event series for Project of the Week.
- Outcome: The Luma event series is created with the correct cover, dates, Slack location, description, public URL, hosts, and reminder emails.
- Trigger: A new Project of the Week is ready to be scheduled.
- Frequency: For each new Project of the Week.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Luma event management, Project of the Week repository, Slack project channel, and event cover image.
- Tools: Luma, GitHub, Slack.
- Inputs: Project title, project repository URL, date range, Slack channel link, description, and cover image.

flat_steps.body_md: The first thing you need to do is open, [Luma](https://lu.ma/home) and select “+Create” to create an event.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows Luma's +Create control on the home page. This starts the event series setup for Project of the Week.

flat_steps.body_md:
On the event tab, change the photo of the event and add the event details (Event name and time).

    Note: Always make sure that the timezone is on CET – Berlin.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Luma event form with the cover, event name, date, time, and timezone fields. It clarifies where to enter the Project of the Week details and confirm the Berlin timezone.

flat_steps.body_md:
After, copy the Event URL from the github repo

    Note: Event URL is an internet address that is composed of the entered data.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the event URL value from the GitHub project page. This URL is reused as the location or public link for the Luma event.

flat_steps.body_md: And paste it under the “Add Event Location”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Add Event Location field in Luma. Paste the project URL there so attendees know where the project activity happens.

flat_steps.body_md:
To proceed, go to the “Add Description” tab of the event and add the description.

    Note: In this example, we copied the previous published event description on Luma with minor changes made since new event is just a continuation.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Add Description area in the Luma event editor. This is where the copied Project of the Week description is added and adjusted.

flat_steps.body_md: In here, change the link in the word “here” to the a new link. Copy the new link from the github repo.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the linked word here inside the Luma description. Update that hyperlink so it points to the current Project of the Week repository or page.

flat_steps.body_md: And then add it.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the link editor used to apply the copied project URL. It confirms the destination is added to the selected description text.

flat_steps.body_md: After, click on “More”, scroll down and enter the public URL of the event and once done, click “Update”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the More section where the public URL can be entered. Updating this field sets the event's public address before saving.
The screenshot shows the Update button after the public URL has been entered. Saving here applies the public URL change to the Luma event.

flat_steps.body_md: After the event has created, go to “Overview”, scroll down and under “Hosts”, add Alexey’s gmail.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Hosts section on the Luma event overview. Add Alexey's Gmail there so he is listed as a host and organizer.

flat_steps.body_md:
Lastly, on the “Emails” tab, click on “+ New Post” and use the template below:
    Hey Everyone! Welcome to day \<1\>of the project of the week: \<Name of the event\>, we're starting soon! The plan for the day can be seen on Slack. You can join the Project of the Week channel using this link. To learn more about the project of the week, check out the video of Antonis (insert link).

    Note: Schedule a post before the actual event. In here, the event occurs on May 23 to 29, 2024. A total of 7 days, therefore, 7 posts for the project of the week event. This serves as a reminder for the attendees of the event.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows Luma's post/email editor with the daily Project of the Week reminder text. Use it as a model for scheduling one message per event day.

prose.body_md:
How to Create Project of the Week Events in Luma
This document shows the steps on how to Create Project of the Week Events in Luma.

Project of the Week description: [https://docs.google.com/document/d/18rCX0Fhkq2KK4pi_lTHMR9yRaIHY2-956zoOyBRsvRE/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/document/d/18rCX0Fhkq2KK4pi_lTHMR9yRaIHY2-956zoOyBRsvRE/edit?usp=sharing)

Step-by-step Instructions

summary.body_md:

- Purpose:

- Frequency:

prerequisites.body_md:

- Access:

flat_steps.body_md: On the Luma event, select “Sessions”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Luma event management tabs with Sessions selected. This is where individual event dates can be edited.
