frontmatter.source: backend/scripts/seed-templates.ts

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Template type: `workshop`
- Trigger: manual
- Task count: 36

- Default assignee ID: `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001`

purpose.body_md:
## Purpose

Preserve the canonical task template in Git so the operational process can be reviewed, searched, and restored independently of the runtime task database.

frontmatter.title: Add email for people who are already on the website
frontmatter.summary: Explains how to add missing email hashes for existing website people before submitting new event or speaker forms.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Website/Add email for people who are already on the website.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: adding people’s email addresses to our website database
- Outcome: sometimes it happens that we have invited somebody previously to our events, but it was some time ago, so we don’t have their email address in our database. We need to save it, because next steps (e.g. adding the event to our website) depend on it.
- Trigger: before we fill in the “people” form to add a new person to our website (see [Create speaker profiles in Airtable](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PaX3fYo7grHvQ2d7Mw1LBXZidJmFXqJ6ttk-DUeLNXM/edit))
- Frequency: As needed per person.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Website repository and Airtable person records.
- Tools: GitHub, SHA-1 hash generator, Airtable.
- Inputs: Person email and website profile ID.

groups.title: Checking if we already have the guest in our database

steps.body_md:
Open the [hashes.csv file](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io/edit/main/scripts/data/hashes.csv) in our [DataTalks.Club’s GitHub repository](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io).

    This document contains the emails of our guests hashed for security (we don’t want to publish their email in public)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows `scripts/data/hashes.csv` opened in the DataTalks.Club website repository. This file stores email hashes next to person IDs without exposing raw email addresses.

steps.body_md:
Check if the person we want to add already exists by typing their ID in the search field.

    To find their ID, refer to this document: [Check if a person already exists on our website and find their ID](../../events/planning/sops/check-if-a-person-already-exists-on-our-website-and-find-their-id.md)

    Example when a person already exists in our database:


    Example when a person doesn’t exist yet:


    If the person is already there, don’t do anything – stop this process and continue with the github actions for adding the event to our website.

    If they don’t exist in our database, proceed further with this process document.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows a successful search result in `hashes.csv` for an existing person ID. If the ID is already present, no new hash entry is needed.
The screenshot shows the same search when no matching person ID is found in `hashes.csv`. That empty result means you should continue with generating and adding the email hash.

steps.body_md: To compute the hash of the guest’s email, visit [http://www.sha1-online.com/](http://www.sha1-online.com/)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the SHA-1 generator site used to convert a guest email into a hash. This is the external tool used before editing `hashes.csv`.

steps.body_md:
After, enter the email of the speaker and make sure that the drag-down button is on “sha-1”

    Note: Make sure that all letters of the email are in lower case and there should no spaces.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the email input and algorithm dropdown set to “sha-1”. Confirm the email is lowercase and the hash type is correct before generating the value.

steps.body_md: Once done, click “hash” and copy the result for sha1

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the generated SHA-1 output after clicking “hash”. Copy this result exactly for the new `hashes.csv` line.

groups.title: Adding the guest – editing the hashes.csv file

steps.body_md: But if they are not, click on the pen edit tool icon.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the GitHub edit pencil for `scripts/data/hashes.csv`. Use it only after confirming the person ID is missing from the file.

steps.body_md:
Paste the copied hash with the name of the speaker’s ID at the end of the files

    Very important: Add a line break after the last hash code. You should see that the next line is empty - in this particular example, the line 55 is there, but there’s nothing.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the new hash and person ID added at the end of `hashes.csv` with an empty line after it. That final blank line keeps the CSV formatted correctly for the website scripts.

steps.body_md: Once done, select “Commit changes”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the GitHub “Commit changes” action for saving the updated hash file. Committing this edit makes the new person email hash available to the website workflow.

steps.body_md: After that, you can run the github actions for adding people to the website from the form

frontmatter.title: Add the link of the stream to the website
frontmatter.summary: Explains how to add a completed YouTube stream link to the website so visitors can watch past events.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Website/Add the link of the stream to the website.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Adding the YouTube link of a past stream to our website
- Outcome: We want people to watch the events after they happened
- Trigger: After the live stream
- Frequency: Per event stream.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Website repository.
- Tools: GitHub.
- Inputs: Stream link and target website section.

flat_steps.body_md: Open [datatalksclub.github.io/\_data/events.yaml](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io/blob/main/_data/events.yaml) on GitHub
Next, click on the pen tool icon on the right side of your screen to edit the document

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the edit control for `_data/events.yaml` in the GitHub web UI. Use it to confirm you are editing the event data file, not just viewing it.

flat_steps.body_md:
After clicking, find the title of the event
    For example, for “Using Data to Create Liveable Cities” this is how the record in the file looks like:

    \- time: 2024-09-02 12:30:00

    title: "Using Data to Create Liveable Cities"

    speakers: \[rachellim\]

    type: podcast

    link: https://lu.ma/l3i43o1e

Under "link:..." create a line break with the format: "youtube: \*insert link\*"

    Example of the result:

    \- time: 2024-09-02 12:30:00

    title: "Using Data to Create Liveable Cities"

    speakers: \[rachellim\]

    type: podcast

    link: https://lu.ma/l3i43o1e

    youtube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VXQIGHUWeL0

    Note: Don't include the ampersand (&) and time of the video in pasting the link. e.g

    youtube.com/watch?v=afasfas&t=3s, don't include "&t=3s"


    Examples of not correct links:

    - [https://youtube.com/watch?v=0Fx5PCoLkf4&t=3s](https://youtube.com/watch?v=afasfas&t=3s)

    - [https://youtu.be/0Fx5PCoLkf4](https://youtu.be/0Fx5PCoLkf4)

    Examples of correct links:

    - [https://youtube.com/watch?v=0Fx5PCoLkf4](https://youtube.com/watch?v=afasfas&t=3s)

    In addition to copying a correct YouTube link, remove the “&s=3s” or lines that follow an ampersand and the time of the video.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot highlights the timestamp portion of a YouTube URL that should not be copied into the `youtube:` field. The website entry should keep only the base watch link.

flat_steps.body_md: Lastly, click "Commit changes"

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows GitHub's “Commit changes” button after editing `events.yaml`. This is the save point that submits the stream link update to the repository.

frontmatter.title: Adding Images to Articles on Our Website
frontmatter.summary: Explains how to add or replace article images on the website and verify the site rebuilds successfully.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Website/Adding Images to Articles on Our Website.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Add or replace images in website articles.
- Outcome: The article references uploaded image files and the site rebuilds successfully.
- Trigger: An article needs a new or updated image.
- Frequency: As needed per article.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Website GitHub repository.
- Tools: GitHub, website build actions.
- Inputs: Article file, image file, image path, and caption.

flat_steps.body_md: The first thing you need to do is Access the [Github repository](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io/tree/main/_posts)

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the website repository opened at the `_posts` directory. This is where article markdown files are located before adding or updating image references.

flat_steps.body_md: And then, locate the article

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the article list in `_posts` so you can select the markdown file that needs the image update. Confirm the filename matches the target article before editing.

flat_steps.body_md: And then, click on the edit icon

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the GitHub edit pencil for the selected article file. This opens the markdown editor where the image URL and figure title will be added.

flat_steps.body_md:
After, go the the “images” file on Github


    And then, go to “posts”

    After, go to the location of the image

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the repository root with the `images` directory. Article assets need to be uploaded there so the website can serve them.
The screenshot shows the `images/posts` folder in GitHub. This folder groups images used by website posts and articles.
The screenshot shows the destination subfolder for the article image inside `images/posts`. Uploading to the matching folder keeps the image path consistent with the article.

flat_steps.body_md: Click on the drop down button and select “Upload file”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows GitHub's Add file menu with “Upload files” selected. Use this control to add the article image to the repository from your computer.

flat_steps.body_md: Once done, click “Commit Changes”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the commit button for the uploaded image file. Committing here saves the new asset before you reference it from the article.

flat_steps.body_md: After, click the file and click copy

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the uploaded image file page where its repository path or URL can be copied. That copied value becomes the image reference in the markdown article.

flat_steps.body_md: After clicking the edit icon on step number 3, paste the copied link of the image and and change the title of the figure in t

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the article markdown editor with the image link and figure title being updated. This is where the uploaded asset is connected to the article content.

flat_steps.body_md: Click “Commit Changes”

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the final GitHub commit action for the edited article markdown. This saves the new image reference so the website build can include it.

prose.body_md:
How to Add Images to Articles on our Website
This procedure will show you the steps on howt o Add Images to Articles on our Website

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Debugging Process for Website Content
frontmatter.summary: Explains how to diagnose failed website content generation, fix the problematic Airtable record, and rerun the workflow.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Website/Debugging Process for Website Content.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Diagnose failed website content generation runs.
- Outcome: The problematic Airtable record is fixed and the workflow reruns successfully.
- Trigger: The website content workflow fails.
- Frequency: As needed.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Website GitHub Actions and content Airtable base.
- Tools: GitHub Actions, Airtable.
- Inputs: Failed workflow logs and affected Airtable records.

flat_steps.body_md: First, open [https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io/blob/main/\_data/events.yaml](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io/blob/main/_data/events.yaml) and then, click on “Actions” to view different workflow runs.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the website repository header with the “Actions” tab available from the `events.yaml` page. That tab is where the content-generation workflow history is reviewed.

flat_steps.body_md: To locate the error, click on “All workflows”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the GitHub Actions sidebar with “All workflows” selected. This view lets you find the failed run before narrowing into a specific workflow.

flat_steps.body_md:
Under “All workflows”, click on the specific workflow that needs debugging.

    Note: A red “x” mark indicates a failed workflow run.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the workflow run list where failed runs are marked with a red status icon. Pick the failing workflow from this list to inspect its logs.

flat_steps.body_md: To view workflow source code, click on the “generate all” button.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the failed “generate all” run inside GitHub Actions. Opening that run exposes the jobs and log sections that identify the content error.

flat_steps.body_md: After clicking, locate which part of the workflow has failed and click to view the code.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the failed job section inside the workflow run. The red marker identifies the step whose logs need to be opened first.

flat_steps.body_md: Then, run through the code and see which part the error occured.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the workflow log output where the error details appear. Use the failing log line to identify which content record or field needs attention.

flat_steps.body_md: After locating the error, visit the website to check the error.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the public website view used to confirm how the logged content problem appears to users. It connects the GitHub Actions error to the affected website section.

flat_steps.body_md:
After which, proceed to the section where the error is.

    Note: In here, error was found at the “Events” section of the Website.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the affected website content area, in this case the Events section. Use it to match the visible problem with the record that needs correction.

flat_steps.body_md:
Then, select the specific row, right-click and select “Delete record” to fix the error.

    Note: There are different ways of fixing errors, it only depends on the nature of the error. Some errors may require updating information in some columns.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Airtable record context menu with “Delete record” available. This is the correction path when the failing content row should be removed instead of edited.

flat_steps.body_md: With the error being fixed, go back to the website repository and click on “Run workflow”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the GitHub Actions “Run workflow” control after the Airtable fix. Rerunning the workflow regenerates the website content from the corrected data.

flat_steps.body_md:
After, wait for the workflow to run.

    Note: An “orange circle” on the right side of the text indicates a running workflow. It may take a few minutes to complete the run.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the workflow in progress with a running status indicator. Wait here until the run finishes before checking whether the fix worked.

flat_steps.body_md:
After a successful run, check on the workflow code to see if the error has been completely fixed.

    Note: A “green checkmark” indicates a successful run of the workflow.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows a completed GitHub Actions run with a green success status. This confirms the regenerated website content no longer fails at the workflow level.
The screenshot shows the successful job details after reopening the workflow run. Use it to verify the previously failing step now completes cleanly.

prose.body_md:
How to Debug Content for Website
In preparing and running content for the Website, specific errors occur at certain parts which need fixing to allow the smooth running of the website.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Extracting Images from Websites
frontmatter.summary: Explains how to extract preview image URLs from web pages and resize them when the source supports it.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Website/Extracting Images from Websites.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Extract preview images from web pages for reuse.
- Outcome: A direct image URL is available, optionally resized when the source supports it.
- Trigger: A page preview image is needed for event or website content.
- Frequency: As needed.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Source web page.
- Tools: Chrome DevTools.
- Inputs: Page URL and desired image size.

flat_steps.body_md: After opening the website, right-click and select “Inspect”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the browser context menu with “Inspect” selected on the source page. This opens DevTools so you can find the page's preview image metadata.

flat_steps.body_md: Then, go to “\<head\>”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the DevTools Elements panel focused on the page's `<head>` section. Preview image metadata is usually stored there rather than in the visible page body.

flat_steps.body_md: Under \<head\>, locate for tech called \<meta name=”image” property=”og:image” content=”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the `og:image` meta tag inside `<head>`. The `content` value is the direct image URL to copy for reuse.

flat_steps.body_md: To proceed, double-click on the link.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the image URL selected in the meta tag's `content` attribute. Double-clicking helps capture the exact link without surrounding HTML.

flat_steps.body_md: On another tab, paste the link on the address bar and enter to view image.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the copied `og:image` URL opened directly in a browser tab. This confirms the link returns the actual image file before you use it elsewhere.

flat_steps.body_md:
On the address bar, adjust the width and height according to preference.

    Note: This process is specific for Luma and will not work for other websites.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the Luma image URL with width and height parameters in the address bar. Adjust those values only when resizing a Luma-hosted image.

prose.body_md:
How to Extract Images from Websites
This document shows the steps on How to Extract Images from Websites.

Step-by-step Instructions

frontmatter.title: Renaming Profile ID on the website
frontmatter.summary: Explains how to fix mismatched website profile IDs so person files, images, and event references resolve correctly.
frontmatter.source: Processes/Website/Renaming Profile ID on the website.docx

summary.body_md:
## Summary

- Purpose: Fix mismatched website person profile IDs.
- Outcome: Person file names, profile IDs, and image names match the event references.
- Trigger: A speaker profile does not resolve correctly on the website.
- Frequency: As needed.

prerequisites.body_md:
## Prerequisites


- Access: Website GitHub repository.
- Tools: GitHub.
- Inputs: Existing person profile, expected person ID, and related image file.

flat_steps.body_md: First, visit datatalks.club/people to view the different profiles.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the public `datatalks.club/people` directory where speaker profiles are listed. Start here to compare the visible profile name with the profile URL.

flat_steps.body_md:
Then, scroll through the list of names and click on the profile that needs editing.

    Note: In here, Tanya Berger-Wolf’s profile name does not match with what is shown on the URL of the website. Instead of Tanya Berger-Wolf, tanyawold is displayed.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows Tanya Berger-Wolf's profile entry in the people list. It identifies the public profile that needs to be checked for an ID or URL mismatch.
The screenshot shows the opened profile page where the URL does not match the displayed speaker name. This confirms the profile ID typo before changing repository files.

flat_steps.body_md: After spotting the problem, visit the Website Repository ([https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/datatalksclub.github.io)) and click on “Code” then, “people”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the website repository's `people` directory in GitHub. This folder contains the person profile files that control website IDs and profile pages.

flat_steps.body_md: After which, scroll through the different profile IDs and look for the ID that needs editing and click.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the list of profile ID files in the `people` directory. Select the file whose ID matches the incorrect public URL.

flat_steps.body_md:
Then, click on “Edit this file” (pen icon) button on the top right corner of the screen.

    Note: In here, renaming is only done because we already have the profile of the person but in instances that we do not have an existing profile, creating a new file is needed.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the GitHub edit pencil on the selected person profile file. Opening the editor lets you fix mismatched fields such as `short`, `title`, or image references.

flat_steps.body_md:
After clicking, edit the details that showed errors.

    Note: In here, both the website URL and image file name have typographical errors and do not match with the “short” and “title” names.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the profile YAML fields being edited for the incorrect person ID. Use it to align the displayed title, short ID, and related filenames.
The screenshot shows the corrected profile details after the typo is fixed in GitHub's editor. Check that the values now match the intended speaker ID before committing.

flat_steps.body_md: After doing all the necessary edits, click on “Commit changes” on the right side of the screen to save the edits.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the “Commit changes” button for the edited person profile file. This saves the profile ID corrections to the repository.

flat_steps.body_md: Since the image file name showed errors, go back to “Code” and click on “images” to view the different image file names of the different accounts.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the repository `images` directory after fixing the profile file. Use it to locate the author image whose filename also needs to match the corrected ID.

flat_steps.body_md: Inside the “images” folder, scroll through the different sub-folders and click on “authors”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the `images/authors` folder in GitHub. Author profile photos are stored here and should use filenames that match the person IDs.

flat_steps.body_md:
Within the “authors” folder are the different images of each Profile in the website, scroll through the names and click on the file that needs editing.

    Note: As seen below, what is displayed is “tanyawold.jpg” instead of “tanyabergerwolf.jpg”.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the author image filenames, including the mistyped `tanyawold.jpg` example. Select the mismatched image file so its name can be corrected.

flat_steps.body_md: After, select the image and then, click “Edit this file” (pen icon) on the right side of the screen and make the necessary edits.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the selected author image file with GitHub's edit control available. Use this when renaming or correcting the image file metadata in the repository UI.
The screenshot shows the edit view for the author image filename after selecting the file. Confirm the new image name matches the corrected profile ID.

flat_steps.body_md:
Then, click on “Commit changes” to save the edits made.

    Note: Double-check to see if all file names match with the Profile ID. Also, it may take some time for the edits to reflect on the website.

screenshots.caption: The screenshot shows the commit action for saving the author image filename change. This final commit keeps the profile file and image asset names in sync.

prose.body_md:
How to Rename the Profile ID on the Website
There are instances where the name in the Profile ID does not match with what is shown on the URL of the website, file names for the images, and on some parts or there are some typographical errors.

Note: This is only for updating file names for existing profiles in the website. In cases where there is no existing profile, creating a new file is needed.

Step-by-step Instructions
